summary: Jeff Atwood critiques a common programming interview question—'how would you write a routine to copy a file?'—by showcasing David Pickett's response that exposes the question's absurdity through increasingly complex edge cases. The back-and-forth reveals how a seemingly simple task involves file attributes, compression, encryption, security implications, and backup software compatibility. Atwood argues that such questions are 'interview riddles' that don't deserve face-value responses. His ultimate point is that competent programmers shouldn't be writing file copy routines at all—they should use existing, tested solutions. The post satirizes the disconnect between artificial interview problems and real-world software engineering judgment.
key_points: Interview questions asking candidates to implement basic utilities like file copying ignore the massive complexity hidden in edge cases
A simple 'copy file' operation involves attributes, compression, encryption, security holes, and backup software compatibility
Good candidates should question the premise of implementing something that already exists
These 'interview riddles' don't test practical programming judgment
The best answer to 'how would you implement X' is often 'why would I?'
money_quotes: The only rational answer to 'how would you write a routine to copy a file?' is another question: why would any competent programmer ever write a file copy routine?
Look, just copy the damn file.
David's answers are even better – they implode the question under its own weight.
'How would you write a routine to copy a file?' is just another interview riddle; it doesn't deserve a face value response.
The last time I checked, moving mount Fuji wasn't a part of our business plan, either.
That could be a serious security hole. Especially if wherever this file copy function ends up supports arbitrary parameters.
key_insight: The best programmers know when not to write code, and interview questions that reward reinventing solved problems test the wrong skills entirely.
